elements.sent-2411-001: In this paper we introduce some recent progresses on the convergence rate in Wasserstein distance for empirical measures of Markov processes.
elements.sent-2411-002: For diffusion processes on compact manifolds possibly with reflecting or killing boundary conditions, the sharp convergence rate as well as renormalization limits are presented in terms of the dimension of the manifold and the spectrum of the generator.
elements.sent-2411-003: For general ergodic Markov processes, explicit estimates are presented for the convergence rate by using a nice reference diffusion process, which are illustrated by some typical examples.
elements.sent-2411-004: Finally, some techniques are introduced to estimate the Wasserstein distance of empirical measures.
elements.sent-2411-006: Keywords: Empirical measure, Wasserstein distance, Markov process, Riemannian Manifold, eigenvalue..
elements.sent-2411-007: The empirical measure is a fundamental statistics to estimate the stationary distribution of a Markov process.
elements.sent-2411-008: In this paper, we study the long time behavior of empirical measures for Markov processes under the Wasserstein distance.
elements.sent-2411-009: For two nonnegative functions $f,g$ on a space $E$, we write $f\lesssim g$ if there exists a constant $c>0$ such that $f\lesssim c g$ holds on $E$, and write $f\asymp g$ if $f\lesssim g$ and $g\lesssim f$.
elements.sent-2411-010: Let $(M,\rr)$ be a Polish space, let $\scr P$ be the space of all probability measures on $M$.
elements.sent-2411-011: For a Markov process $X_t$ on $M$, the empirical measure is defined as \begin{equation}\mu_t:=\ff 1 t \int_0^t \dd_{X_s}\d s\end{equation} for $t>0$, where $\dd_{X_s}$ is the Dirac measure at $X_s$.
elements.sent-2411-012: We intend to study the long time behavior of $\mu_t$ under the $p$-Wasserstein distance for any $p\in [1,\infty)$: \begin{equation}\W_p(\mu_1,\mu_2):= \inf_{\pi\in \scr C(\mu_1,\mu_2)}\bigg( \int_{M\times M} \rr^p\d\pi\bigg)^{\ff 1 p},\ \ \mu_1,\mu_2\in \scr P,\end{equation} where $\scr C(\mu_1,\mu_2)$ is the set of all couplings of $\mu_1$ and $\mu_2$.
